input: What is recursion in Python and why is a base case needed?
actual_output: Recursion is when a function calls itself. A base case stops the repeated calls, preventing infinite recursion.
expected_output: Recursion is a technique in which a function calls itself. A base case defines when the recursion stops and prevents infinite calls.
retrieval_context: A recursive function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem.
Every recursive algorithm needs a base case that stops further recursive calls; without one, recursion continues until the runtime limit is reached.

input: Can a Python list be changed after it is created?
actual_output: Yes. Python lists are mutable, so their elements can be added, removed, or replaced after creation.
expected_output: Yes, lists are mutable and can be modified after creation.
retrieval_context: Lists are mutable Python collections. Items may be replaced, appended, or removed after a list is created.
